### AF 2024-08-14 1374

(03:54:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued the [[Republican Party]] had intentionally neglected its white, male base to pander to corporations, the [[American Israel Public Affairs Committee|AIPAC]], and diverse voter blocs, treating its core constituency as a "liability."

### AF 2024-08-13 1373
(00:00:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced a formal declaration of war against the [[Republican Party]] and the presidential campaign of [[Donald Trump]].

### AF 2024-08-13 1373
(00:01:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated his ultimate goal was the reelection of [[Donald Trump]] but intended to use his leverage to urge the campaign to adopt nationalist priorities.

### AF 2024-08-13 1373
(00:05:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes began supporting [[Donald Trump]] in March 2016, primarily due to [[Donald Trump|Trump's]] position against mass immigration, which he viewed as a global demographic transition transforming [[Western civilization]].

### AF 2024-08-13 1373
(00:07:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes supported [[Donald Trump]] for his opposition to foreign wars and free trade, which he collectively termed "globalism."

### AF 2024-08-13 1373
(00:08:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes supported [[Donald Trump]] for his opposition to never-ending [[Middle East]] wars in [[Iraq]], [[Afghanistan]], and [[Syria]].

### AF 2024-08-13 1373
(00:09:26) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified mass immigration, foreign wars, and free trade as the three core issues that differentiated [[Donald Trump]] from the [[Republican Party]].

### AF 2024-08-13 1373
(00:10:06) In 2016, Nicholas J. Fuentes volunteered for the [[Donald Trump]] campaign, organizing college students in [[Boston]] to door-knock in [[Manchester, New Hampshire]].

### AF 2024-08-13 1373
(00:10:50) Throughout [[Donald Trump]]'s first term, Nicholas J. Fuentes hosted a show called [[United States|America]] First, initially on the [[Right Side Broadcasting Network]].

### AF 2024-08-13 1373
(00:11:28) After the 2020 election, Nicholas J. Fuentes organized or participated in "Stop the Steal" rallies in [[Lansing]], [[Atlanta]], [[Harrisburg]], [[City of Phoenix|Phoenix]], and [[Washington D.C.]].

### AF 2024-08-13 1373
(00:12:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes attended the January 6th rally at the [[United States Capitol]], which he described as following [[Donald Trump]]'s directive from a speech on the ellipse.

(00:12:54) Following January 6th, Nicholas J. Fuentes had $500,000 in cash seized, was placed on a no-fly list by the [[U.S. Department of Homeland Security]], and was subpoenaed by the January 6th [[U.S. Congress|House]] Select Committee.

(00:13:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes was banned from [[Instagram]], [[Facebook]], [[DLive]], multiple airlines, banks, and credit card payment processors due to his attendance at the January 6th rally.

(00:14:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he had never been arrested, charged, or interviewed by the [[Federal Bureau of Investigation]] or any other federal law enforcement agency.

(00:18:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed personnel from the campaigns of [[Marco Rubio]], [[Ted Cruz]], [[George W. Bush]], and [[Jeb Bush]] were hired into the [[Donald Trump]] administration.

### AF 2024-08-13 1373

(00:29:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that after the [[City of Charlottesville|Charlottesville]] rally in August 2017, [[JD Vance]] disavowed neo-[[Nazi Party|Nazis]] and the far-right on [[Warner Bros. Discovery|CNN]].

(00:12:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes declares a "Groyper War II" against the [[Donald Trump]] campaign, citing a series of policy failures including [[Donald Trump]]'s promise to hire [[Nikki Haley]], his support for stapling green cards to foreign diplomas, his disavowal of [[Project 2025]], and threats to bomb [[Iran]].

(00:26:53) Nicholas J. (00:36:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes states that the "Groyper War" is necessary because the [[Donald Trump]] campaign is being run by "never-Trumpers" like [[Susie Wiles]] and [[Chris LaCivita]], and if [[United States|America]] First supporters are not welcomed, the campaign does not deserve to win.

### AF 2024-08-09 1371

Fuentes argues that the [[Joe Biden]] administration skillfully navigated the crisis by deploying an unprecedented amount of [[U.S. Department of Defense|U.S. military]] hardware to the [[Middle East]] as a deterrent and promoting the narrative that the [[Tehran]] assassination was a covert operation, not an airstrike.

### AF 2024-08-09 1371
(00:57:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes credits the [[Joe Biden]] administration with preventing a regional war by deterring [[Iran]] with a massive [[U.S. Department of Defense|U.S. military]] deployment, the largest since the 2003 invasion of [[Iraq]].

(01:02:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes claims that [[Iran]] and its proxies, including [[Hezbollah]] and the [[Houthis]], have shown tremendous restraint over the past year, avoiding escalation because they do not want a confrontation with the [[United States]].

(01:04:14) Nicholas J. Fuentes argues that the foreign policy goal of the [[United States]], [[Iran]], and [[Lebanon]] is to restrain [[Israel]], prevent a regional war, and push for a ceasefire in [[Gaza Strip|Gaza]].

(01:06:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes challenges the characterization of [[Iran]] as an irrational "theocracy," pointing out that [[Saudi Arabia]] is an absolute monarchy and that [[Iran]]'s proxies have shown disciplined restraint, unlike a suicidal regime.

(00:07:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed frustration that commentators like [[Eva Vlaardingerbroek]] and [[Sargon]] were praising [[Vladimir Putin]]'s historical lecture while only concluding that Western leaders like [[Joe Biden]] were dumb, which he found intellectually bankrupt.

### AF 2024-02-10 1290
(00:11:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes compared the reaction to the [[Vladimir Putin]] interview to the fawning comments sections of old [[Christopher Hitchens]] and [[Richard Dawkins]] debates or the [[Ben Shapiro]] and [[Jordan Peterson]] interviews, labeling it a "mid-wit impulse."

### AF 2024-02-10 1290
(00:14:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Vladimir Putin]]'s long historical answer was a "hostile rhetorical tactic" and a "power move" meant to put [[Tucker Carlson]] off balance, not a profound legal case for the war in [[Ukraine]].

### AF 2024-02-10 1290
(00:19:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that [[Vladimir Putin]] did not say anything new in the interview, offered no new information or arguments about the war in [[Ukraine]], and simply recited the official [[Russian]] position.

### AF 2024-02-10 1290
(00:23:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the [[Vladimir Putin]] interview as a "big nothing burger" that changed nothing and suggested [[Vladimir Putin]] had "punked" [[Tucker Carlson]].

### AF 2024-02-10 1290
(00:24:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that [[Tucker Carlson]] had attempted to interview [[Vladimir Putin]] a year prior but aborted it after being tipped off that the intelligence community was spying on his communications.

### AF 2024-02-10 1290
(00:25:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes summarized that the [[Vladimir Putin]] interview was about two hours and ten minutes long and began with a straightforward question from [[Tucker Carlson]] about why [[Russia]] invaded [[Ukraine]] in February 2022.

### AF 2024-02-10 1290
(00:27:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the real reason [[Russia]] invaded [[Ukraine]] was the prospect of [[Ukraine]] joining [[North Atlantic Treaty Organization|NATO]] and the [[Europe|European Union]], not the 1,000-year historical narrative [[Vladimir Putin]] presented.

### AF 2024-02-10 1290
(00:28:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the war in [[Ukraine]] was motivated by contemporary domestic security concerns, specifically preventing [[North Atlantic Treaty Organization|NATO]] military bases and intermediate-range missiles from being deployed unacceptably close to [[Russia]].

### AF 2024-02-10 1290
(00:30:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes reiterated his view that [[Vladimir Putin]]'s lengthy historical answer was a "hostile tactic" and a "power move" similar to a comedic bit by [[Samuel J. Hyde|Sam Hyde]] about telling long stories to frustrate debt collectors.

### AF 2024-02-10 1290
(00:32:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed out that when [[Tucker Carlson]] asked if [[Hungary]] then had a claim to western [[Ukraine]] based on similar historical logic, [[Vladimir Putin]] dismissed the question, proving the historical argument was not the real basis for the invasion.

### AF 2024-02-10 1290
(00:35:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that state borders are ultimately defined by a centralized state's ability to project power and control its periphery, not by idealistic constructs of history or national identity.

### AF 2024-02-10 1290
(00:36:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed the standard [[Russian]] justifications for the war that [[Vladimir Putin]] repeated, including the denazification of [[Ukraine]], broken promises about [[North Atlantic Treaty Organization|NATO]] expansion, the 2008 Bucharest conference, the 2014 Maidan coup, and the prospect of [[Russia]] joining [[North Atlantic Treaty Organization|NATO]] under [[Bill Clinton]].

### AF 2024-02-10 1290
(00:38:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the [[Vladimir Putin]] interview as a "perfunctory" and "scripted" corporate-style press release format rather than an open, vulnerable, or intimate journalistic interview.

### AF 2024-02-10 1290
(00:40:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that the [[Vladimir Putin]] interview's intended audience was likely the [[Russian]] population, as a display of strength and condescension towards the Western media, rather than an attempt to persuade an [[United States|American]] audience.

### AF 2024-02-10 1290
(00:42:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes reflected that working with [[Kanye West]] made him realize the dissident right lacked imagination and forward-thinking vision, being stuck in reflexive cynicism and a "politics of subtraction."

(00:30:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes recalled seeing a suspicious man on a media tower directing people into the [[United States Capitol|Capitol]] on [[January 6th Attack|January 6th]], whose identity was never uncovered.

(00:35:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes, who attended all the preceding Stop the Steal rallies, framed [[January 6th Attack|January 6th]] as the culmination of six years of [[Donald Trump|Trump]] supporters feeling systematically screwed out of democracy and representation.

### AF 2023-09-20 1220
(00:36:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed the ways [[Donald Trump]] was "screwed" by the system, including [[FISA]] court spying during the election, media opposition, the [[Billy Bush]] tape, congressional [[Republican Party|Republicans]], the [[COVID-19]] pandemic, and finally being cheated in the 2020 election.

### AF 2023-09-20 1220

### AF 2023-09-20 1220
(00:38:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes believed [[January 6th Attack|January 6th]] represented a point of no return; if the situation wasn't clarified there, [[Joe Biden]] would become president and everything that followed would happen.

(00:40:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the "[[fed]] op" narrative obfuscates and diminishes the significance of [[January 6th Attack|January 6th]], reducing it to a story about instigators rather than widespread political fury over a stolen election.

(00:45:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that if the mythology of [[January 6th Attack|January 6th]] as a legitimate revolt is taken away, it becomes impossible to talk seriously about winning in 2024 against a rigged system.

(00:46:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes compared the "[[fed]] op" narrative around [[January 6th Attack|January 6th]] to claiming [[Donald Trump]]'s entire political rise was an "[[Israel|Israeli]] op," noting that while there may be elements of truth, the powerful narrative of a popular revanchist movement is what matters.

### AF 2023-09-20 1220
(00:49:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes moved to the featured story on the government shutdown, explaining the need for a stopgap continuing resolution (CR) by September 30th to fund the government while 12 appropriations bills are negotiated.

### AF 2023-09-20 1220
(00:50:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that five members of the [[House Freedom Caucus]] scuttled a procedural vote on an appropriations bill, preventing it from reaching the floor as part of an ongoing battle with [[Kevin McCarthy]].

### AF 2023-09-20 1220
(00:54:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained that if the [[House Freedom Caucus]] withheld votes, [[Kevin McCarthy]]'s only alternative to a shutdown was to caucus with [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] via the "Problem Solvers Caucus" to pass a clean CR with no [[Republican Party|Republican]] demands.

### AF 2023-09-20 1220
(00:54:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that if [[Kevin McCarthy]] worked with [[Democratic Party|Democrats]], he would face a motion to vacate to remove him as Speaker.

### AF 2023-09-20 1220

(00:59:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized [[Kevin McCarthy]] for making commitments to hold [[Joe Biden]] accountable when elected Speaker, then completely folding when [[Republican Party|Republicans]] had leverage over the debt ceiling.

### AF 2023-09-20 1220

(01:02:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes framed the conflict as [[Kevin McCarthy]] being forced into a catch-22: fight the [[House Freedom Caucus]] for stronger demands or work with [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] for a bill with no concessions.

### AF 2023-09-20 1220
(01:03:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that if [[Kevin McCarthy]] passed a bill with [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] that gave [[Joe Biden]] everything he wanted, it would demonstrate there was no point in electing [[Republican Party|Republicans]], aside from the 15 protesting [[House Freedom Caucus]] members.

### AF 2023-09-20 1220
(01:04:48) Nicholas J. (00:22:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that [[Oliver Anthony]]'s rise directly correlated with [[United States|America]]'s decline, stating "[[United States|America]]'s being killed every [[time]] that song plays."

(00:32:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the "death of the [[country]]" is visible in these daily degradations of public life and civility, not just in major geopolitical events like losing a war.

(00:33:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that much of the perceived racial dimension of societal decay is attributable to certain groups more frequently engaging in low-class behaviors, not their race itself.

(00:34:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that internalizing the media's "low class vulgar caricature of white people" is "the most anti-white thing that a white [[person]] in this day and age can do."

(00:38:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes said that when [[Donald Trump]] descended the escalator to announce his candidacy, he represented a powerful, masculine, successful white man with no white guilt, which was a "stark contrast" to the rest of the [[Republican Party]].

(00:41:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes agreed with [[Charlie Kirk]]'s description of certain populist figures as a "professional activist class of... basically losers" whose whole identity is politics.

(00:47:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes called for abandoning "vulgar mass consumer culture," anti-intellectualism, and low-class behaviors like wearing gym shorts in public, which he associated with a "wigger" identity.

### AF 2023-09-15 1217
(00:47:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained that DACA, or Deferred Action on Childhood Arrivals, was created by [[Barack Obama]] ten years ago to protect illegal immigrants who arrived as minors from deportation.

### AF 2023-09-15 1217
(00:48:19) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that DACA recipients are often called "Dreamers," with approximately 800,000 people protected by the program, most of whom are now adults in their 30s.

### AF 2023-09-15 1217
(00:49:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized DACA as "little more than a completely illegal reclassification" that arbitrarily shields a group of illegal immigrants from deportation.

### AF 2023-09-15 1217
(00:50:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted that in the fall of 2017, [[Donald Trump]] floated giving DACA recipients citizenship, and in January 2018 proposed a deal for their permanent residency in exchange for $20 billion for a border wall and merit-based immigration reform.

### AF 2023-09-15 1217
(00:51:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Donald Trump]]'s proposed 2018 immigration deal, which included amnesty for DACA recipients, reduced legal migration, and border wall funding, failed to pass despite [[Republican Party|Republican]] control of [[Congress]] and the [[Joe Biden|White House]].

### AF 2023-09-15 1217
(00:52:22) Nicholas J. Fuentes compared [[Donald Trump]]'s failed 2018 DACA compromise to [[Ronald Reagan]]'s immigration amnesty, which also promised but never delivered concomitant border security.

### AF 2023-09-15 1217
(00:55:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the DACA case would likely be appealed to the [[United States|U.S]]. Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit and then to the [[Supreme Court]].

(00:57:04) Nicholas J. ### AF 2023-08-25 1204

### AF 2023-08-25 1204
(01:17:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the political establishment, including the media, federal law enforcement, and intelligence agencies, had collaborated in a multi-year campaign against [[Donald Trump]] since the 2016 [[Republican Party|Republican]] primary.

### AF 2023-08-25 1204
(01:17:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the use of FISA courts in June 2016 to spy on [[Donald Trump|Trump]] Tower, the appointment of Special Counsel [[Robert Mueller]], two impeachment attempts, and the events of [[January 6th Attack|January 6th]] as key escalations in this campaign.

### AF 2023-08-25 1204
(01:17:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the 2020 election as having been rigged against [[Donald Trump]].

### AF 2023-08-25 1204
(01:17:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that [[Donald Trump]] raised a direct challenge against the [[United States|American]] "deep state" and regime upon taking office.

### AF 2023-08-25 1204
(01:17:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that an editorial in *[[The New York Times]]* openly admitted to deep state actors sabotaging the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration from within.

### AF 2023-08-25 1204
(01:17:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the criminal charges against [[Donald Trump]], including those in [[Manhattan]], over classified documents, and under RICO in [[Fulton County]], were politically motivated "lawfare" over technicalities rather than serious crimes.

### AF 2023-08-25 1204
(01:17:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Donald Trump]]'s refusal to concede the 2020 election and his continued political activity forced the establishment to escalate to criminal indictments.

### AF 2023-08-25 1204
(01:17:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed the establishment's strategy shifted to attempting to imprison [[Donald Trump]] after failing to defeat him in primaries, general elections, impeachments, or through censorship.

(01:17:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested racial dynamics were at play, with [[black]] officials in [[Fulton County]] processing [[Donald Trump]] in a manner akin to how they believe the system treats [[black]] defendants.

(01:17:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a *[[The New York Times|New York Times]]* report stating [[Donald Trump]]'s booking took only 20 minutes, faster than for most defendants, and that he listed his weight as 215 pounds, 24 pounds less than a 2018 [[Joe Biden|White House]] doctor's report.

### AF 2023-08-25 1204
(01:17:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that during his official [[Joe Biden|White House]] photo shoot, [[Donald Trump]] scowled at the camera and told aides he thought he looked like [[Winston Churchill]].

(01:17:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes predicted the mugshot would backfire on the establishment by becoming an iconic symbol of resistance and martyrdom for [[Donald Trump]]'s movement.

### AF 2023-08-25 1204
(01:17:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the mugshot perfectly encapsulated the narrative of an oppressive system targeting a patriotic outsider, fulfilling the prophecy [[Donald Trump]] had made since 2015.

### AF 2023-08-25 1204
(01:17:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the establishment was in a "knife fight" with [[Donald Trump]] and was forced to take drastic, public action because he posed an existential political threat to them.

(01:17:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that if [[Donald Trump]] were not imprisoned, he would win the 2024 election, "decapitate" the administrative state by firing and replacing tens of thousands of federal workers, and destroy the careers of his opponents.

(01:17:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the situation as a "damned if they do, damned if they don't" double bind for the establishment, orchestrated by [[Donald Trump]]'s political strategy.

### AF 2023-08-25 1204

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes began his broadcast on a Thursday evening, announcing the show's featured stories would focus on [[Afghanistan]] and foreign policy.

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:02:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he was "kind of rooting for the [[Afghanistan|Taliban]]," comparing his feeling to when he saw [[Donald Trump|Trump]] defeating the establishment in the 2016 [[Republican Party|GOP]] primaries.

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:04:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes drew a parallel between the current situation in [[Afghanistan]] and the rise of [[Islamic State of Iraq and Syria|ISIS]] in [[Iraq]] following [[Barack Obama]]'s withdrawal of troops in 2011, suggesting the media coverage of [[Afghanistan|Taliban]] brutality could be a pretext for the [[United States|U.S]]. to remain engaged.

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:08:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes recalled that under the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration in 2019, a monthly apprehension of around 150,000 immigrants was considered a scandal, but numbers had since grown much worse.

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:09:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that the broadcast date, August 12th, was the fourth anniversary of the 2017 [[City of Charlottesville|Charlottesville rally]] in [[State of Virginia|Virginia]].

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:10:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes attended the [[City of Charlottesville|Charlottesville rally]] on Saturday, August 12, 2017, for the Lee Park demonstration, but was not present for the prior night's Tiki Torch march.

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:11:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes was invited to [[City of Charlottesville|Charlottesville]] by [[Faith Goldy]], who was with [[Rebel Media]] at the [[time]], and he flew out last-minute to protest what he termed "white replacement" and the removal of the Robert E. Lee Monument.

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:12:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes believed the [[City of Charlottesville|Charlottesville rally]] was intended to reunite a right wing that had splintered after the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] election, citing divisions involving figures like [[Mike Cernovich]], [[Jeff Giza]], and [[Richard Spencer]].

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:13:08) The [[City of Charlottesville|Charlottesville rally]] was universally condemned and became a significant stain on Nicholas J. Fuentes's career, making him "radioactive" and limiting his opportunities for years.

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:13:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that his attendance at [[City of Charlottesville|Charlottesville]] was necessary to later mainstream ideas about demographic change, as talking about "white genocide" was taboo at the [[time]].

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:14:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the people talking about demographic replacement in 2017, such as [[Richard Spencer]] and [[Chris Cantwell]], as "toxic," "dysfunctional," or potentially infiltrated by federal agents.

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:15:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed to 2021 mainstream media headlines about [[United States|America]] diversifying and the white population shrinking as validation of the concerns voiced at [[City of Charlottesville|Charlottesville]] four years prior.

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:17:05) At the [[City of Charlottesville|Charlottesville rally]], Nicholas J. Fuentes met several figures for the first [[time]], including [[Timothy Gionet|Baked Alaska]], [[Millennial Matt]], [[Bryden]], [[Brittany Venti]], and [[James Allsup]].

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:17:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes promoted his Telegram and [[Gab]] accounts, noting he was about to reach 100,000 followers on [[Gab]] less than a year after creating the account.

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:18:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed the [[Anti-Defamation League|ADL]] was responsible for getting him banned from [[Twitter]] in July, after years of pressure from the [[Southern Poverty Law Center|SPLC]] and other groups.

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:19:39) Within about a month of being banned from [[Twitter]], Nicholas J. Fuentes gained nearly 100,000 followers on [[Gab]] and achieved similar or better engagement on that platform.

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:20:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes compared the growth of his platform, [[AmericaFirst.live]], to his previous bans on [[Google|YouTube]] and [[DLive]], noting it took eight months to reach 65,000 followers on his own site.

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:21:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes mocked critics like [[Megan Squire]] and outlets like the [[The New York Times|New York Times]], [[The Wall Street Journal|Wall Street Journal]], and [[National Public Radio|NPR]] for their efforts to deplatform him.

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:22:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes recounted that in the spring and summer of 2019, during the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration, illegal border crossings reached a high watermark of around 180,000 per month, which he considered a major betrayal.

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:25:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that by the end of the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration, the border was effectively closed and immigration was under control, a record he felt was still contested by some.

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:28:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes predicted that based on the record highs in spring and summer, illegal immigration would reach unprecedented levels in the fall when weather conditions improved for travel.

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:31:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained that many illegal immigrants deliberately surrender at ports of entry, knowing loopholes in the law and training from NGOs will likely result in their release into the [[country]] under catch-and-release policies.

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:34:00) With 212,000 people apprehended in July, and many more not apprehended, Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded [[the United States]] had "completely open borders."

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:35:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued the dramatic difference in border numbers proved that electing [[Republican Party|Republicans]] like [[Donald Trump|Trump]] made a substantial difference, contrary to claims that both parties deliver the same outcomes on immigration.

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:36:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated the difference between a [[Donald Trump|Trump]] and [[Joe Biden|Biden administration]] was about a quarter of a million illegal immigrants every month, which would have significant electoral and societal impacts over [[time]].

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:38:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that the crisis at the border, combined with inflation, censorship, and surging crime, demonstrated that abdicating from politics was "demonstrably ridiculous."

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:38:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes warned that if current immigration trends continued, it would become impossible for [[Republican Party|Republicans]] to win national elections in states like [[Arizona]], [[State of Nevada|Nevada]], [[State of Colorado|Colorado]], [[State of Florida|Florida]], or [[State of Texas|Texas]].

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:39:44) With apprehensions nearing a million for the year and numbers increasing monthly, Nicholas J. Fuentes questioned what could reverse the trend, suggesting no upper limit existed for how many people could cross into [[United States|America]].

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:40:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes advised viewers that given the accelerating crises in immigration, inflation, crime, and government weaponization, individuals needed to focus on protecting themselves and their families by "moving to higher ground."

(00:42:19) Nicholas J. Fuentes used the analogy of a nightclub fire to describe the national situation, urging viewers to recognize the obvious danger and calmly make their way to safety despite others' complacency.

### AF 2021-08-12 0859
(00:45:05) Nicholas J. Fuentes questioned how anyone could view the influx of hundreds of thousands of illegal immigrants monthly as sustainable or not detrimental to the [[country]], stating "we're the only ones that have a serious answer... which is shut down all the immigration."

(00:56:47) Nicholas J. (01:04:44) Nicholas J. ### AF 2021-04-14 0792

### AF 2021-04-13 0791

(00:18:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes quoted [[Tucker Carlson]]'s monologue as stating, "Demographic change is the key to the [[Democratic Party]]'s political ambitions," and that [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] plan to change the [[country]]'s population to make existing voters irrelevant.

### AF 2021-04-13 0791

(00:06:58) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that if the [[State of Florida|Florida]] Big Tech bill was weak, it would set a poor precedent for similar legislation in other states.

### AF 2021-04-12 0790
(00:16:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes began analyzing the [[State of Florida|Florida]] Big Tech censorship bill, acknowledging it as a positive step and the most significant [[Republican Party|Republican]] attempt to date, but highlighting concerns raised by figures like [[Laura Loomer]] and [[Michelle Malkin]].

### AF 2021-04-12 0790
(00:17:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed the [[State of Florida|Florida]] Big Tech bill represented a more effective attempt to stop censorship than efforts by [[Donald Trump]], [[Josh Hawley]], or any other federal official.

### AF 2021-04-12 0790
(00:17:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes recalled that [[Ron DeSantis]] had promised the bill would pass "no problem" during a speech at [[Conservative Political Action Conference|CPAC]].

### AF 2021-04-12 0790
(00:17:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained that the [[State of Florida|Florida]] bill proposed a $100,000 daily fine for platforms that deplatform statewide political candidates, but criticized this as financially negligible for trillion-dollar companies like [[Facebook]] and [[Google]].

### AF 2021-04-12 0790
(00:18:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued the bill's narrow scope, protecting only statewide candidates, and its weak penalties rendered it ineffective against major tech companies.

### AF 2021-04-12 0790
(00:18:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes proposed amending the [[State of Florida|Florida]] bill to protect independent journalists and citizen journalists, not just political candidates.

### AF 2021-04-12 0790
(00:18:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes advocated for the [[State of Florida|Florida]] bill to be broadened to apply not just to social media platforms but also to ISPs, telecommunications companies, and financial services like [[Peter Thiel|PayPal]] and [[Stripe]].

### AF 2021-04-12 0790
(00:19:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that an article on the [[State of Florida|Florida]] bill mentioned concerns raised by [[Paul Gosar]], [[Michelle Malkin]], [[Laura Loomer]], and former [[Donald Trump|Trump administration]] officials, but omitted his own criticisms.

### AF 2021-04-12 0790
(00:20:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes reiterated that the proposed $100,000 daily fine per candidate in the [[State of Florida|Florida]] bill was insufficient to change the behavior of companies like [[Facebook]] and [[Google]].

### AF 2021-04-12 0790
(00:20:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes used [[Laura Loomer]] as an example of a candidate who would be protected under the [[State of Florida|Florida]] bill's narrow terms if she were banned from a platform.

### AF 2021-04-12 0790
(00:21:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that fines in the [[State of Florida|Florida]] bill needed to be increased to at least $1 million per day to have a deterrent effect on massive tech corporations.

### AF 2021-04-12 0790
(00:22:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed out that the number of people protected by the [[State of Florida|Florida]] bill—statewide candidates—was so small that even maximum fines would inflict negligible financial pain on tech companies.

(00:22:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that even if every prospective candidate in [[State of Florida|Florida]] were banned and fined against, the total penalty would be easily absorbed by [[Facebook]] and [[Google]].

### AF 2021-04-12 0790
(00:23:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes proposed increasing the fine in the [[State of Florida|Florida]] bill to $1 million or even $100 million per day, citing [[Laura Loomer]]'s suggestion and the example of effective legislation in [[Europe]].

### AF 2021-04-12 0790
(00:24:20) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized a provision in the [[State of Florida|Florida]] bill that required consistent application of censorship standards, arguing it tacitly endorsed censorship rather than banning it for legally protected speech.

### AF 2021-04-12 0790
(00:24:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes advocated for the [[State of Florida|Florida]] bill to explicitly protect all speech covered by the [[Concept|First Amendment]], forcing platforms to align with constitutional principles.

### AF 2021-04-12 0790
(00:25:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[State of Florida|Florida]] bill must be expanded to cover financial deplatforming by services like [[Peter Thiel|PayPal]] and [[Stripe]], which he stated control 97% of online payment gateways and from which he is banned.

### AF 2021-04-12 0790
(00:26:59) Nicholas J. Fuentes called for the [[State of Florida|Florida]] bill to include retroactive protection, requiring companies to reinstate previously banned accounts and face fines for every day they refuse.

### AF 2021-04-12 0790
(00:27:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated the [[State of Florida|Florida]] bill should protect current office holders, not just candidates, and criticized its exclusion of journalists, private citizens, and public officials.

### AF 2021-04-12 0790

(00:52:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that knowledge of inherent differences between racial groups and genders is empirically verifiable through data, research, and lived experience, but is suppressed by ideological conformity.

### AF 2021-04-12 0790

(00:45:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that such policies were creating a racial caste system where non-white people received special benefits and status conferred by the government, funded by white taxpayers.

### AF 2021-03-25 0780
(00:46:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that racial conflict is an inevitable result of diverse groups living in the same polity and competing for the same public resources, leading to resentment over unequal outcomes.

### AF 2021-03-25 0780

### AF 2021-03-25 0780

### AF 2021-03-25 0780

(00:53:44) Nicholas J. ### AF 2021-02-22 0764

(00:45:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes predicted the [[Joe Biden]] administration would use the [[Federal Bureau of Investigation|FBI]], [[Internal Revenue Service|IRS]], and Big Tech to persecute and silence its political opponents.

(00:49:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes analyzed [[Joe Biden]]'s cabinet, noting his Chief of Staff was on the board of the [[Walton Foundation]], his Defense Secretary was on the board of [[Raytheon Technologies Corporation|Raytheon]], and his [[Treasury Department|Treasury]] Secretary was a former Chair of the [[Federal Reserve]], calling them the epitome of the [[Washington D.C.|Washington]] revolving door.

(00:50:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes acknowledged real [[China|Chinese]] infiltration and subversion in the [[United States]] but argued domestic corruption from [[United States|American]] oligarchs like [[Mark Zuckerberg]], [[Jeff Bezos]], [[Sheldon Adelson]], and [[Robert Mercer]] was a more immediate problem.

### AF 2021-01-20 0746

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(00:52:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the threat to [[United States|America]] is multi-layered, involving the [[Israel lobby]], [[Jews|world Jewry]], [[China|Chinese]] infiltration, the [[Federal Reserve]], and elite institutions like [[Harvard University|Harvard]] and the [[Rhodes Scholarship]].

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(00:52:57) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that the fundamental problem is the corrupt [[United States|American]] regime, which, if sound, would be more resistant to foreign infiltration from countries like [[China]].

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(00:53:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that, given a choice, he would prefer [[China|Chinese]] censorship rules, which might forbid criticism of [[Xi Jinping]], over [[United States|American]] censorship enforced by groups like [[Antifa]], [[Black Lives Matter]], the [[Anti-Defamation League|ADL]], and the [[Southern Poverty Law Center|SPLC]].

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(00:54:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that as [[United States|American]] global power diminishes, dissenters could find recourse under the protective umbrella of regional hegemons, creating a multipolar world less dominated by the [[United States|American]] regime.

(00:56:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested that countries like [[China]] and [[Russia]], which are against the [[United States|American]] regime, are in a sense aligned with domestic dissenters who are also against that regime, though he clarified he was not colluding with foreign powers.

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(00:57:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes accused [[Mark Zuckerberg]] of putting $350 million into an illegal "get out the vote" effort targeting heavily [[Joe Biden|Biden]] precincts, and cited [[Twitter]] and [[Google|YouTube]]'s suppression of the [[Hunter Biden]] story as examples of [[United States|American]] companies rigging the election.

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(01:00:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed that he would miss [[Donald Trump]]'s charismatic personality and "Trumpisms," contrasting it with the "cringe" politics of the [[Joe Biden]] era.

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(01:02:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that hierarchical societies with followers and leaders are the natural state of human civilization, from the pyramids to the present, and that the goal should be to get the right people in power rather than awakening the masses.

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(01:06:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that in an ideal hierarchical civilization, people would slavishly praise the [[king]] and God, rather than contemporary celebrities, and expressed a desire for an [[United States|American]] monarchy with [[Donald Trump]] as [[king]].

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(01:07:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Donald Trump|Trump]]'s 2016 victory showed people's desire for a "[[king]]-like" leader who protects the kingdom's people, reflecting a deep longing for monarchy over liberal democracy.

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(01:09:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes posited that human beings are designed to be governed by a [[king]], reflecting the "kingdom of heaven," and that this is embedded in human conscience and biology.

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(01:10:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained that the [[United States|America]] First movement is a specific response to the problems of the past 30 years, including immigration changes since 1965, free trade starting under [[Ronald Reagan|Reagan]] and [[Clinton]], and foreign wars since the 1990s.

### AF 2021-01-20 0746

(01:12:29) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed [[Patrick Buchanan|Pat Buchanan]], [[Lou Dobbs]], and [[Ron Paul]] as figures who discussed [[United States|America]] First themes like nationalism and opposition to globalization prior to [[Donald Trump|Trump]].

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(01:13:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[United States|America]] First is a refutation of globalization, which only fully emerged after the [[Cold War]] with the creation of supranational institutions like the [[International Monetary Fund|IMF]], [[World Bank Group|World Bank]], and [[WTO]].

### AF 2021-01-20 0746
(01:15:34) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that many potential [[United States|America]] First supporters voted for [[Barack Obama]] in 2008 due to his populist promises on ending wars and bank bailouts, and only turned to [[Donald Trump|Trump]] after [[Obama]]'s perceived failure.

(00:05:59) Nicholas J. (00:12:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes predicted the [[Joe Biden|Biden]] administration and future [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] administrations would not regulate or break up [[Google]] but would instead pressure it to enforce stricter hate speech regulations.

(00:13:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that January 19, 2021, was the final night of the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration, with [[Joe Biden]] and [[Kamala Harris]] set to be inaugurated for the next four years.

(00:14:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[Joe Biden|Biden]]-[[Kamala Harris|Harris]] administration was one that "nobody really wanted," noting that [[Kamala Harris|Harris]] was less popular than [[Joe Biden|Biden]] in the primary.

(00:15:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized [[Joe Biden|Biden]]'s cabinet nominees, including a defense secretary who worked on the board of [[Raytheon Technologies Corporation|Raytheon]] and [[treasury]] secretary [[Janet Yellen]], as representing special interests and lobbyists.

### AF 2021-01-19 0745
(00:17:10) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration as having "come in like a lion and out like a lamb," a theme of disappointment for the show.

### AF 2021-01-19 0745
(00:18:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[Donald Trump|Trump]] administration's final pardons and farewell address represented a "final humiliation, betrayal, and disappointment."

(00:21:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that 74 million people voted for [[Donald Trump|Trump]] in 2020 and many still believe in the promise of Trumpism, which must be used as a practical tool to sustain the movement.

(00:23:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Donald Trump|Trump]] was planning to unveil over a hundred pardons and sentence commutations on his final full day, a unilateral power he had used less frequently than his predecessors.

### AF 2021-01-19 0745

(00:28:56) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported a rumor that [[Donald Trump|Trump]] wanted to pardon [[Julian Assange]] and [[Steven Crowder|Steve Bannon]] but was talked out of it by advisors after [[Mitch McConnell]] threatened to allow [[Donald Trump|Trump]]'s conviction in the [[Senate]] impeachment trial.

(00:33:30) Nicholas J. (00:55:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the "mythology" of the 2016 campaign showed that "one man can make all the difference" and that [[United States|America]] is not dead, which people need to believe in during the difficult [[Joe Biden|Biden]] years.

(00:56:41) Nicholas J. Fuentes recalled that in 2015, the expected presidential contest was between [[George W. Bush|Jeb Bush]] and [[Hillary Clinton]], and [[Donald Trump|Trump]]'s unexpected rise disrupted the system and caused a "mass awakening."

### AF 2021-01-04 0740

### AF 2021-01-04 0740
(00:07:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the post-2020 election period would initiate a four-year battle for succession within the [[Republican Party]], pitting the populist, nationalist ideology of [[Donald Trump]] against the party's neoconservative and neoliberal establishment.

### AF 2021-01-04 0740
(00:10:08) Nicholas J. Fuentes framed the coming intra-party conflict as a rehash of the 2016 primary, with [[Donald Trump]] representing populist, nationalist, trade protectionist, military non-interventionist, and immigration restrictionist policies against the [[Republican Party|GOP]] establishment.

### AF 2021-01-04 0740
(00:12:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that figures including [[Dan Crenshaw]], [[Nikki Haley]], [[Ted Cruz]], [[Donald Trump]], [[Donald Trump Jr.]], and [[Kimberly Guilfoyle]] were campaigning in [[Country of Georgia|Georgia]] for [[Republican Party|Republican]] [[Senate]] candidates [[Kelly Loeffler]] and [[David Perdue]].

### AF 2021-01-04 0740
(00:13:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes identified himself, [[Michelle Malkin]], and streamers on [[DLive]] including Jaden, Jake, Steve, Vince, Patrick, and Scott as the only prominent conservative voices encouraging voters to stay home from the [[Country of Georgia|Georgia]] runoff.

### AF 2021-01-04 0740
(00:13:36) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the primary political task was to unseat [[Mitch McConnell]] and the [[Republican Party|Republican]] establishment and replace them with "[[United States|America]] First" figures, which required making them lose elections when they cannot be primaried.

### AF 2021-01-04 0740
(00:15:39) Nicholas J. Fuentes explained that if [[Joe Biden]] were inaugurated, [[Kamala Harris]] would become [[Vice]] President and president of the [[Senate]], giving [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] a de facto majority via her tie-breaking vote if the chamber were tied 50-50.

### AF 2021-01-04 0740
(00:27:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued there was no meaningful policy difference between the [[Republican Party|Republican]] and [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] parties on issues like [[Concept|Big Tech]] regulation, endless foreign wars, immigration, and COVID-19 stimulus, rendering the "hold the line" argument for voting [[Republican Party|Republican]] moot.

### AF 2021-01-04 0740
(00:31:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that [[Donald Trump]] won the November 3 election, expanding his electoral college margin from 2016 and becoming the largest vote-[[getter]] for an incumbent president in history, but was cheated out of victory.

### AF 2021-01-04 0740
(00:32:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed the [[Republican Party]] failed in its responsibility to provide adequate poll watchers in swing states, which allowed voter fraud to occur after the November 3 election.

### AF 2021-01-04 0740
(00:34:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[Republican Party]] had multiple legal and constitutional opportunities to keep [[Donald Trump]] in office but systematically refused to act because the party establishment wanted him removed.

### AF 2021-01-04 0740
(00:35:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed the [[Republican Party]] used [[Donald Trump]] to achieve policy goals like corporate tax cuts, military budget expansion, and support for [[Israel]], but was ready to discard him once those were accomplished.

### AF 2021-01-04 0740
(00:36:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Republican Party|Republican]] politicians betrayed their voters for the past two months with no recourse because voters had no leverage, such as being willing to withhold votes.

### AF 2021-01-04 0740
(00:38:13) Nicholas J. Fuentes presented two scenarios: electing [[Kelly Loeffler]] and [[David Perdue]] would preserve the [[Republican Party|Republican]] [[Senate]] majority but grant the establishment more power with no price for their betrayals, while letting them lose would send a message and create leverage for change within the party.

### AF 2021-01-04 0740
(00:40:54) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that if the [[Republican Party|Republican]]-controlled [[Congress]] did not repeal Section 230 protections for [[Concept|Big Tech]] or build a border wall when they had full control of government, they would never do it under a [[Joe Biden]] administration.

### AF 2021-01-04 0740
(00:42:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that the chance of achieving policy goals with the current [[Republican Party|Republican]] and [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] parties was zero, whereas creating a crisis for the [[Republican Party]] by losing the [[Country of Georgia|Georgia]] runoff presented a non-zero chance for future change.

### AF 2021-01-04 0740
(00:45:43) Nicholas J. Fuentes took credit for being one of the only major voices, alongside [[Michelle Malkin]] and attorney [[Lin Wood]], encouraging voters to boycott the [[Country of Georgia|Georgia]] [[Senate]] runoff.

### AF 2021-01-04 0740
(00:48:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes claimed that any commentator shilling for [[Kelly Loeffler]] and [[David Perdue]] in [[Country of Georgia|Georgia]] was likely being paid by donors or interests within the [[Republican Party|GOP]] system, which would cut off funding if they advocated for withholding votes.

### AF 2021-01-04 0740
(00:50:50) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that the unified support for [[Kelly Loeffler]] and [[David Perdue]] from figures like [[Dan Crenshaw]] and [[Nikki Haley]] indicated the political "system" wanted them elected, which was opposite to the "[[United States|America]] First" movement's goal.

### AF 2021-01-04 0740
(00:53:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed out that if [[Donald Trump]] and [[Mike Pence]] remained in office, [[Republican Party|Republicans]] would already have a [[Senate]] majority via Pence's tie-breaking vote, making the [[Country of Georgia|Georgia]] runoffs less critical; therefore, the party's failure to fight for [[the White House]] revealed their priority was removing [[Donald Trump|Trump]], not keeping the [[Senate]].

(01:09:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes believed it was "extremely unlikely" that [[Mike Pence]] would take unprecedented action to not count certain votes or that enough [[Republican Party|Republican]] senators would join the objection to force a contingent election.

(01:12:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that if [[Republican Party|Republican]] officials truly wanted [[Donald Trump]] to win, they would have pressured state legislatures to appoint alternate electors in November or December, not just filed amicus briefs or announced objections in January.

(01:13:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that senators like [[Ted Cruz]] and representatives like [[Mo Brooks]] and [[Paul Gosar]] were among the few in [[Congress]] who genuinely wanted [[Donald Trump]] to remain president.

(43:24:27) Nicholas J. Fuentes viewed [[Donald Trump]]'s actions in his final weeks—fighting for more stimulus, withdrawing troops, protecting Southern [[heritage]], and targeting [[Concept|Big Tech]]—as showing his true colors, but wished this approach had been taken four years earlier.

(57:01:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Donald Trump]] started a movement on immigration, foreign policy, and shifting the Overton Window that would be difficult for the establishment to undo, and his followers needed to learn from and emulate his success.

Fuentes stated that the COVID-19 stimulus bill passed that day included direct cash payments of $600, extended unemployment benefits at $300, and provided hundreds of millions of dollars to foreign countries including $500 million for [[Israel]] and $700 million for [[Republic of the Sudan|Sudan]].

(00:31:00) Nicholas J. ### AF 2020-12-21 0737
(01:02:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Turning Point USA]]'s purpose was to steer young conservatives into supporting the political status quo, channeling them into organizations like the [[Heritage Foundation]] or staff positions with establishment [[Republican Party|Republicans]].

(04:21:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that a signature audit alone would not catch other alleged fraud methods, such as out-of-state voting or duplicate ballots, which had been cited in [[State of Nevada|Nevada]].

(04:27:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes quoted [[Brad Raffensperger]] as saying the audit was needed to restore confidence in the system, and [[Gabriel Sterling]] as blaming [[Republican Party|Republicans]] for spreading 2020 election disinformation and [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] for doing the same in 2016 and 2018.

### AF 2020-12-17 0735
(04:43:98) Nicholas J. Fuentes asserted that the purpose of the audit, as presented by [[Brad Raffensperger]] and [[Gabriel Sterling]], was to silence dissent rather than to investigate fraud and overturn the election results.

### AF 2020-12-17 0735
(06:26:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the failure to overturn the election results represented a "systemic failure" and "systematic betrayal" by the [[Republican Party]] at every level of government.

### AF 2020-12-17 0735
(06:44:74) Nicholas J. Fuentes contended that if [[Republican Party|Republican]] officials believed a [[Democratic Party|Democratic]] [[Senate]] would be catastrophic, they would have fought with equal intensity to keep [[Donald Trump]] in [[the White House]], which they did not.

### AF 2020-12-17 0735
(07:13:38) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Joe Biden]] and [[Kamala Harris]] would control the [[Central Intelligence Agency|CIA]], [[Federal Bureau of Investigation|FBI]], [[NSA]], [[Internal Revenue Service|IRS]], [[U.S. Department of Homeland Security|DHS]], [[U.S. Department of State|State Department]], and [[U.S. Department of Defense|DOD]], and that [[Republican Party|Republican]] acquiescence to the election result indicated they were "okay with all of that."

### AF 2020-12-17 0735
(07:31:12) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[Republican Party|GOP]]'s push for the [[Country of Georgia|Georgia]] [[Senate]] runoff was an attempt to assimilate voters back into the political system after their failure to keep [[Donald Trump]] in power.

### AF 2020-12-17 0735
(07:36:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes suggested that 74 million [[Donald Trump|Trump]] voters could secede from the [[Republican Party]] to form a new "MAGA" or "[[United States|America]] First" party.

### AF 2020-12-17 0735
(07:47:52) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that while he was loyal to [[Donald Trump]], he did not feel obligated to support [[Donald Trump|Trump's]] endorsements of figures like [[Brian Kemp]], [[David Perdue]], or [[Kelly Loeffler]], whom he viewed as having betrayed [[Donald Trump|Trump]].

### AF 2020-12-17 0735
(08:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes returned to the topic of COVID-19 vaccines, reporting that the [[Pfizer]] and [[Moderna]] vaccines were being rolled out and citing stories of severe side effects.

### AF 2020-12-17 0735
(08:39:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes read a news report stating two healthcare workers at [[Bartlett Regional Hospital]] in [[Juneau]], [[Alaska]], had concerning reactions minutes after receiving the [[Pfizer]] vaccine, with one hospitalized.

### AF 2020-12-17 0735
(08:59:94) Nicholas J. Fuentes described the case of a middle-aged female healthcare worker with no allergy history [[who]] suffered a severe, recurring anaphylactic reaction requiring intensive care after receiving the [[Pfizer]] vaccine.

### AF 2020-12-17 0735
(09:27:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the vaccine was developed in only nine months, was an experimental gene therapy, and was linked in some reports to potential sterilization.

### AF 2020-12-17 0735
(09:45:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes reported that [[Twitter]] announced it would remove posts spreading "disinformation" about the vaccine, which he characterized as an active cover-up of vaccine skepticism.

### AF 2020-12-17 0735
(10:11:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes predicted that vaccine mandates would become enforced through "immunity passports," restricting access to jobs, schools, and travel for those [[who]] refused the shot.

### AF 2020-12-17 0735
(10:52:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Bill Gates]] had been a mega-donor to the [[World Health Organization]] and had practiced vaccination programs in [[African continent|Africa]] for decades, suggesting a long-term agenda behind vaccine pushes.

### AF 2020-12-17 0735
(11:24:22) Nicholas J. Fuentes declared he would never take any vaccine, including the COVID-19 vaccine, and would move to another state if necessary to avoid mandates, prioritizing his "biological integrity."

### AF 2020-12-15 0734
(00:00:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes hosts the nightly political commentary program *[[United States|America]] First*.

### AF 2020-12-15 0734
(00:03:06) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced that [[AFPAC|AFPAC 2]] would be held in [[City of Orlando|Orlando]], [[State of Florida|Florida]] at the end of February 2021.

### AF 2020-12-15 0734
(00:03:40) Nicholas J. Fuentes opened an internship application on his website, [[NicholasJFuentes.com]], seeking volunteers to help the [[United States|America]] First movement, specifically for roles in the 2022 elections.

### AF 2020-12-15 0734
(00:06:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated his intention to play a major role in the 2022 elections by primarying [[Republican Party|Republican]] incumbents, vetting candidates, and helping to get "[[United States|America]] First" candidates into office.

### AF 2020-12-15 0734
(00:07:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed disappointment over the Electoral College vote outcome but argued a contested [[Joe Biden]] victory rejected by [[Republican Party|Republicans]] could ultimately benefit the [[United States|America]] First movement by delegitimizing the [[Republican Party]].

### AF 2020-12-15 0734
(00:12:30) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Israel]] is an ally of the [[United States|American]] "regime" and its institutions like the [[Council on Foreign Relations]] and [[AIPAC]], rather than an ally of [[Donald Trump]] or his voters.

### AF 2020-12-15 0734
(00:14:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized evangelical [[Christianity|Christians]] for sending billions in private charity to [[Israel]], a [[country]] he stated did not stand by [[Donald Trump]] during the election dispute.

### AF 2020-12-15 0734
(00:15:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited police arrests of individuals like the McCloskeys in [[City of St. Louis|St. Louis]] and [[|Proud Boys]] in [[Washington D.C.|Washington, D.C.]] as evidence that institutions like the police do not support [[Donald Trump|Trump]] voters.

### AF 2020-12-15 0734
(00:16:55) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the [[U.S. Department of Defense|U.S. military]] as a "mercenary army" that serves defense contractors, the military-industrial complex, and foreign interests rather than the [[United States|American]] people.

### AF 2020-12-15 0734
(00:17:51) Nicholas J. Fuentes advocated for pardoning individuals like [[Julian Assange]] and [[Edward Snowden]] because their actions damage intelligence agencies like the [[Central Intelligence Agency|CIA]] and [[NSA]], which he views as hostile.

### AF 2020-12-15 0734
(00:19:23) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Benjamin Netanyahu|Bibi Netanyahu]]'s quick alignment with the [[Joe Biden]] transition demonstrated loyalty to the permanent "system" rather than to [[Donald Trump]].

### AF 2020-12-15 0734
(00:20:44) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed [[Sheldon Adelson]] and [[Jared Kushner]] among those deserving of conservative ire for betraying the movement, alongside [[Democratic Party|Democrats]] and [[George Soros]].

### AF 2020-12-15 0734
(00:22:42) [[Nick Sandmann]], the Covington [[Catholic Church|Catholic]] student, publicly expressed support for [[Mitch McConnell]] and [[Amy Coney Barrett]], which Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized as being co-opted by the establishment.

### AF 2020-12-15 0734
(00:26:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes raised concerns that a virtual inauguration reliant on broadcast media and potentially deepfake technology could undermine public confidence in the event's reality.

### AF 2020-12-15 0734
(00:29:50) [[Joe Biden]] promised a 100-day mask mandate following his inauguration, which Nicholas J. Fuentes predicted would extend COVID-19 restrictions indefinitely.

### AF 2020-12-15 0734
(00:32:35) Nicholas J. Fuentes warned that "immunity passports" would be introduced, requiring proof of vaccination for activities like air travel, dining indoors, and using ride-sharing services.

### AF 2020-12-15 0734
(00:33:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced reports of plans to inject invisible ink alongside vaccines as a method of verification, which he opposed as a form of coercion.

### AF 2020-12-15 0734
(00:34:39) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated the COVID-19 vaccine was being administered by the military and developed by companies like [[Pfizer]], which he argued should not be trusted due to past issues like the opioid crisis.

### AF 2020-12-15 0734
(00:35:25) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized limited-government conservatives and libertarians for their silence on mandatory vaccination proposals while previously opposing government spending programs.

### AF 2020-12-15 0734
(00:36:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes compared the government's response to COVID-19 to its response to [[September 11 attacks|9/11]], predicting the pandemic would be used to justify a greater expansion of state power and surveillance.

### AF 2020-12-15 0734
(00:38:42) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued the COVID-19 pandemic helped facilitate [[Joe Biden]]'s election victory and would serve as a pretext for implementing a "totalitarian system."

### AF 2020-12-15 0734
(00:45:46) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited the National Defense Authorization Act, which included the [[Elizabeth Warren|Warren]] Amendment removing Confederate names from bases and prevented [[Donald Trump]] from withdrawing troops from [[Afghanistan]], as an example of [[Mitch McConnell]]'s betrayal.

(00:52:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Mitch McConnell]] wanted to avoid a [[Senate]] vote on election objections to conceal that the [[Republican Party]] would vote to certify [[Joe Biden]]'s victory.

Fuentes characterized as an attempt to trick voters into maintaining a [[Republican Party|Republican]] majority.

### AF 2020-12-15 0734
(00:55:48) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that Senators [[Marco Rubio]], [[John Cornyn]], and [[Thom Tillis]] were ready to work with [[Joe Biden]] on amnesty and that [[Marco Rubio]] supported statehood for [[Puerto Rico]].

(01:00:22) Nicholas J. Fuentes listed [[Dan Crenshaw]], [[Charlie Kirk]], the [[The Daily Wire|Daily Wire]], [[Ben Shapiro]], and [[Mitch McConnell]] as opponents of his call to defeat [[Republican Party|Republicans]] in the [[Country of Georgia|Georgia]] [[Senate]] runoffs.

(01:03:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized [[Turning Point USA]] for raising millions of dollars while, in his view, failing to mobilize effectively against election fraud or COVID-19 restrictions.

(00:03:04) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the lawsuit filed by [[State of Texas|Texas]] against [[State of Michigan|Michigan]], [[Wisconsin]], [[Commonwealth of Pennsylvania|Pennsylvania]], and [[Country of Georgia|Georgia]] was a political scam by [[Republican Party|Republican]] officials to appear supportive of [[Donald Trump]] without taking substantive action to overturn the election.

(00:05:21) Nicholas J. Fuentes announced that [[AFPAC|AFPAC 2]], the [[AFPAC|America First Political Action Conference]], would be held in [[City of Orlando|Orlando]], [[State of Florida|Florida]] in February 2021 and would be open to the general public for the first [[time]].

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(00:08:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes opened an application on his website, [[NicholasJFuentes.com]], to recruit interns and staff to help scale up the [[United States|America]] First operation, with the goal of running candidates in the 2022 elections.

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(00:11:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes traveled to [[Washington D.C.|Washington, D.C.]] for a rally on the weekend of December 12-13, which he described as potentially the second-largest rally since the Million MAGA March, though it was marked by confusion due to multiple competing events.

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(00:14:49) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that he and other [[United States|America]] First speakers were not allowed on the main stages at the MAGA March or Jericho March events in [[Washington D.C.|Washington, D.C.]], forcing them to carve out their own speaking area at Freedom Plaza.

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(00:15:03) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted that he had traveled to rally at state capitals in [[City of Phoenix|Phoenix]], [[Harrisburg]], [[Atlanta]], and [[City of Lansing|Lansing]] during the Stop the Steal effort but was not permitted to speak at the official [[Washington D.C.|Washington, D.C.]] events.

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(00:16:52) At the [[United States|America]] First rally in [[Washington D.C.|Washington, D.C.]], speeches were given by Nicholas J. Fuentes, [[Jake Lloyd]], [[Steven Crowder|Steve Franzen]], [[Scott Greer]], Patrick, Jaden, and Vince.

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(00:17:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes praised Jaden for delivering a powerful, high-energy speech at the [[Washington D.C.|Washington, D.C.]] rally, which he considered one of the best he had seen him give.

(00:20:47) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that a planned event on Sunday in [[Washington D.C.|Washington, D.C.]] with [[Alex Jones]] did not materialize due to organizational confusion and lack of clear details from the organizers.

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(00:22:28) A clip from Nicholas J. Fuentes's [[Washington D.C.|Washington, D.C.]] rally speech, in which he declared "we are going to destroy the [[Republican Party|GOP]]," went viral on [[Twitter]], receiving five million views and being retweeted by [[Ilhan Omar]] and several [[Republican Party|congressmen]].

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(00:23:37) Nicholas J. Fuentes clarified that his call to "destroy the [[Republican Party|GOP]]" meant politically decapitating the [[Republican Party]] leadership by primarying figures like [[Mitch McConnell]] and [[Mike Johnson|Kevin McCarthy]] and replacing them with [[United States|America]] First politicians.

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(00:25:16) Nicholas J. Fuentes contrasted the [[United States|America]] First rally, which he organized with minimal resources, against the expensive, large-scale productions of the MAGA March and Jericho March, arguing that his movement's compelling message allowed it to "steal all the thunder."

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(00:26:53) Nicholas J. Fuentes characterized the organizers of the MAGA March as "grifters" and their speaker lineup as consisting of token [[black]] conservatives, token female conservatives, and "[[Turning Point USA]] C-list E-celebrities."

(00:27:54) During his speech, Nicholas J. Fuentes led the crowd in booing and chanting "shame" at [[Lauren Boebert|Lady Maga]], a [[Donald Trump|Trump]]-supporting conservative drag queen, after spotting her in the audience.

(00:29:43) [[Brandon Straka]] and other gay conservatives criticized Nicholas J. Fuentes online after the incident where he led a crowd in booing [[Lauren Boebert|Lady Maga]] at the [[Washington D.C.|Washington, D.C.]] rally.

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(00:30:07) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that with the Electoral College vote completed on December 14th, the strategic purpose of holding rallies at state capitals to pressure legislators to appoint electors had ended, likely concluding that chapter of the Stop the Steal effort.

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(00:34:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes reiterated his view that the [[State of Texas|Texas]] lawsuit was destined to fail because it was filed a day after the Safe Harbor deadline and less than a week before the Electoral College vote, leaving insufficient [[time]] for the [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] to adjudicate it.

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(00:45:01) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] failed in its duty by refusing to hear the [[State of Texas|Texas]] case, despite what he described as obvious evidence of the "biggest voter fraud in [[United States|American]] history" that threatened to install an illegitimate president.

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(00:46:33) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited [[Kamala Harris]]'s accusations against [[Brett Kavanaugh]] during his confirmation hearings and the contentious confirmation of [[Amy Coney Barrett]] as reasons why these justices should have shown loyalty to [[Donald Trump]] by intervening in the election case.

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(00:48:19) Nicholas J. Fuentes concluded that the [[United States|American]] political system was "completely bankrupt" and that real power resided with the media, bureaucrats, and congressional staffers, rather than the courts, elected representatives, or the [[White House]].

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(00:52:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that even when [[Republican Party|Republicans]] control the [[White House]], [[Senate]], [[House]], and [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]], the system fails to deliver policy victories for their voters, citing failures to fund a border wall or reform Section 230 as examples.

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(00:53:39) Nicholas J. Fuentes advocated for [[Republican Party|Republican]] voters to withhold their votes in the [[Country of Georgia|Georgia]] [[Senate]] runoff elections as a strategy to weaken [[Mitch McConnell]] and the [[Republican Party]] establishment, forcing them to earn back support by representing the voters' interests.

(00:57:17) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the next major deadline in the election process was [[January 6th Attack|January 6th]], when the [[Vice President]] would count the Electoral College votes before a joint session of [[Congress]].

(00:58:09) Nicholas J. Fuentes noted the historical symmetry that [[Joe Biden]]'s 306 electoral votes were the inverse of [[Donald Trump]]'s 306 electoral votes in the 2016 election against [[Hillary Clinton]].

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(00:59:02) Nicholas J. Fuentes criticized [[The New York Times]] and other media outlets for describing the Electoral College vote as affirming the result of a "free and fair election," calling it clear editorializing.

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(01:08:22) If no candidate achieves 270 electoral votes on [[January 6th Attack|January 6th]], the election would be decided by a contingent election in the [[House of Representatives]], where each state delegation gets one vote, a process Nicholas J. Fuentes noted would favor [[Donald Trump]] as [[Republican Party|Republicans]] control more state delegations.

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(01:08:45) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed pessimism about overturning the election results, citing the unprecedented nature of such an action and the limited [[time]] remaining, but committed to fighting until the inauguration on January 20th.

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(01:10:02) A [[Senator]] could object to the counting of a state's electoral votes on [[January 6th Attack|January 6th]], forcing a debate and vote in [[Congress]], which Nicholas J. Fuentes identified as another potential, though unlikely, path to challenge the outcome.

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(01:10:28) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that the most likely outcome was that [[Mike Pence]] would count all the electoral votes cast on December 14th, leading to [[Joe Biden]]'s inauguration on January 20th, a result he attributed to [[Republican Party|Republican]] inaction.

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(01:11:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that the election being "stolen" from [[Donald Trump]] created a major opportunity for the [[United States|America]] First movement to galvanize the [[Republican Party|Republican]] base and lead a grassroots takeover of the [[Republican Party]] establishment in the 2022 elections.

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(01:12:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Donald Trump]]'s 2016 victory did not include replacing the [[Republican Party|RNC]] apparatus or installing a new generation of [[United States|America]] First legislators, a task he believed might only be possible following the perceived betrayal of the 2020 election.

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(01:13:11) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed frustration that [[Donald Trump]]'s reelection, which he believed was won "fair and square," was thwarted despite [[Republican Party|Republican]] control of key state legislatures and a favorable [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] composition following the death of [[Ruth Bader Ginsburg]].

### AF 2020-12-14 0733
(01:14:24) Nicholas J. Fuentes framed the next four years as a period of "revenge" against the [[Republican Party]] establishment rather than the [[Democratic Party]], arguing that their betrayal during the election necessitated a political reckoning.

(00:35:31) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed skepticism that the [[State of Texas|Texas]] election lawsuit would succeed, noting it was filed after the safe harbor deadline and just days before the Electoral College vote.

(00:48:46) Nicholas J. (00:50:18) Nicholas J. Fuentes speculated that the [[State of Texas|Texas]] lawsuit was a [[Republican Party]] ploy to salvage their [[Senate]] majority in the [[Country of Georgia|Georgia]] runoffs by pretending to support [[Donald Trump]] while ensuring his defeat.

(01:12:00) The [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]] ruled six to three to hear the [[State of Texas|Texas]] lawsuit, but Nicholas J. Fuentes noted [[time]] was running out before the [[Electoral College]] vote on Monday.

### AF 2020-12-09 0731
(01:14:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that if the election outcome was not overturned, it would be because the [[Republican Party]] and officials in state legislatures, the [[Supreme Court of the United States|Supreme Court]], and the party apparatus chose to forfeit the presidency.

(01:20:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a tweet from [[Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez]] asking if anyone was archiving "[[Donald Trump|Trump]] sycophants" for future accountability, which he interpreted as a threat to use information against [[Donald Trump|Trump]] supporters.

(01:22:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes referenced [[Wajahat Ali]], a writer for the [[The New York Times|New York Times]], [[who]] stated the [[Republican Party|GOP]] must be "broken, burned down, and rebuilt" and that its members should be treated as "active threats to democracy."

(01:25:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes quoted [[Brandon Friedman]], a former [[Obama]] administration official and columnist for the [[The New York Times|New York Times]], [[who]] argued against reconciling with "deplorables" and prioritized reaching out to victims of policies supported by [[Donald Trump|Trump]] voters.

### AF 2020-12-09 0731
(01:25:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Joe Biden]]'s message of healing contrasted with rhetoric from media figures, government officials, and future [[Joe Biden|Biden]] administration personnel [[who]] were calling for persecution of [[Donald Trump|Trump]] supporters.

### AF 2020-12-09 0731
(01:27:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that figures like [[Cynthia Johnson]], [[Stacey Abrams]], and [[Andrew Gillum]] represented a future where more non-white officials, feeling "uppity" and "protected," would make threats against political opponents with increasing impunity.

(01:34:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes advised viewers to prepare for community defense and self-sufficiency in the face of potential civil unrest or state persecution, suggesting they buy firearms, ammunition, and network with trusted neighbors.

(01:35:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated he was not inciting violence but advocating for defensive preparedness against potential illegal violence from groups like [[Antifa]] and [[Black Lives Matter]], or from the government itself.

(01:38:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes warned against joining centralized militias or organizations, advising that community defense should be local, low-key, and optical to avoid being targeted by the [[Federal Bureau of Investigation|FBI]] or other agencies.

(01:40:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes cited a video of a [[U.S. military]] soldier saying he would shoot [[|Proud Boys]] and people refusing to wear masks, arguing this exemplified why [[Donald Trump|Trump]] supporters could not rely on the military or police for protection.

### AF 2020-12-09 0731
(01:45:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes expressed distrust for illegal immigrants [[who]] join the [[U.S. military]], arguing they have no allegiance to the historic [[United States|American]] nation and could be used as mercenaries against the populace.

### AF 2020-12-09 0731
(01:51:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes pointed to the [[LAPD]] academy graduation photos, noting the lack of white recruits, as an example of how police forces may not side with the "historic [[United States|American]] nation" in a conflict.

(01:52:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes compared the modern [[U.S. military]] filled with foreigners to the late [[Rome|Roman Empire]]'s army, suggesting it could lead to a similar downfall.

(02:00:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes argued that [[Google|YouTube]]'s policy of promoting "authoritative information" from mainstream media like [[NBC]], [[CBS News|CBS]], and [[Vox]] meant it was acting as a kingmaker, deciding what is true and controlling all public information.

(02:06:00) Nicholas J. Fuentes stated that [[Right Side Broadcasting Network|RSBN]] ([[Right Side Broadcasting Network]]) could no longer post on [[Google|YouTube]] without risk of termination, effectively silencing a channel with one million subscribers.

### AF 2020-12-09 0731
